data.title: Stocks Shook Off The March Dip: Now Q1 Earnings And April Data Take Center Stage
data.description:
Stocks pace for one of their best April performances on record. The 9% S&P 500® pullback from January through the March 30 intraday low may feel like a distant memory for investors. Still, geopolitical risks remain, and macro unease leaves many pundits doubting the rally’s durability. Is a pause in order? Or are we off to the races like we were a year ago?​

Earnings Beat Rates Signal Underlying Corporate Strength​

The answer may lie in key earnings reports over the next few weeks, along with a trove of macro data points that will set the stage for the summer. On the former front, BofA noted that a solid 76% of S&P 500 companies that have already reported topped Wall Street earnings estimates. That's above the typical 68% Week 1 beat rate. Heavy hitters from the Mag 7 kick off the heart of the reporting period—Tesla (TSLA) is on for tonight.​

We’ll be all over the revenue and income data, as well as crucial guidance numbers as they roll in. It's also important to keep an eye on the macro situation at home and abroad. Of course, the conflict in Iran is A-1 material, but incoming March and April economic updates may soon garner increasing attention. Let’s double-click on that situation to help frame both the earnings backdrop and a busy corporate event season already in full swing.​

Data on Tap, And Why They Matter​

Following Tuesday’s March Retail Sales report, Thursday’s set of S&P Global Flash PMIs will be the timeliest vibe check yet. These Purchasing Manager Index readings are for this month and follow a record-setting (in a bad way) April preliminary reading from the University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers. Its sentiment gauge was the worst on record. ​

That’s saying something, as “UMich” dates back to the middle of the last century. The eye-popping report made for the most K-shaped of all macro stats: the S&P 500 hit a record high as consumer sentiment sat at a record low (but at least they are still going to the movies!).​

Consumers Spent Heavily in March (Whether They Wanted to or Not)

UMich Sentiment Plunges to a Record Low

For it to be official, Friday’s UMich survey reading must confirm the preliminary poll from April 10. Given some optimism about a ceasefire deal between the U.S. and Iran (and the potential reopening of the Strait of Hormuz), households’ financial angst could ease.

April Data Offer Post-War Clues​

To be clear, PMIs and consumer sentiment are “soft” data. They are not “hard” data, such as Retail Sales. Additional hard data hits next Tuesday morning, right before major tech companies post Q1 earnings. ​

Two timely data points that point to a still-decent macro backdrop are the ADP Employment Change Weekly and the Johnson Redbook Retail Sales. ADP’s weekly job change gauge tallied its best level over its brief history (since September 2025) earlier this month, while the weekly consumer spending pulse suggests a steady 7% YoY consumption trend. Not bad.​

Marrying the Macro & Micro​

Those are unofficial barometers, but along with updates from management teams at industry conferences and Annual General Meetings, they may help portfolio managers piece together a macro and micro mosaic. Later in the session on Tuesday, April 28, comes The Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence survey. ​

For background, UMich tends to reflect inflation perceptions, while The Conference Board’s questionnaire leans more on the labor market outlook. With a generally steady jobless rate and real wage growth now near the flat line, it’s difficult to be all that rosy about the April report’s possible findings.​

February Personal Income Turned Negative

The Fed Meets Again. Powell’s Last Stand? Warsh Warming Up.​

From there, the Fed meets mid-week and announces its April policy decision next Wednesday afternoon. No rate change is expected. What’s more, this is not a “dot plot” meeting, so fireworks should be few. ​

FOMC drama centers not so much on Chair Powell right now as on his presumptive successor, Kevin Warsh. The former voting member began his confirmation hearing before the Senate Banking Committee yesterday. While confirmation hearings for Federal Reserve chairs are usually staid events, this one has been a bit more lively, shall we say.​

Next Thursday brings another inflation update, this time the PCE Price Index for March. If it feels like we are getting one major macro data point after another, you’re not crazy. Data from last year’s government shutdown is just now getting back on a regular schedule. A soft March PCE print may help tee up a possible Fed rate cut later this year. ​

Recall that the market-implied odds of an ease were nixed as oil soared above $100 per barrel last month. But as energy prices ebb, rate-cut probabilities have ticked back up.​

April CPI Skyrocketed Due to Oil’s Spike; Core Inflation Remains Tame

From there, the first Friday of May brings a first look at Q1 GDP. This will follow a weak third update to the Q4 economic growth report, so it may carry added market influence.​

Q4 GDP: Tepid 0.4% Growth Rate

April Employment & Berkshire Without Buffett In Charge​

After that, it’ll be all about the labor market. The official April jobs report arrives on May 8, which follows “Woodstock for Capitalists” on May 2 (Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.A) annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, Nebraska).

Low Initial Claims, Falling Continuing Claims Point to Labor Market Improvement

The Bottom Line

It’s a busy macro stretch as company earnings reports come in fast and furious. A focus on real data and earnings may be a welcome development for investors wary of geopolitical headlines. The team at Wall Street Horizon will keep you up to speed with the latest trends, and you can access our industry-leading forward-looking corporate event data to stay ahead of markets.

Original Post

data.title: Rayonier Advanced Materials: Priced For A Success That Isn't There Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Rayonier Advanced Materials Inc. (RYAM) has moved from low $3s to high $11s in the last one year — a sharp 3-4x move driven by expectations of recovery, which is in turn driven by pricing gains in cellulose specialties and expected positive free cash flow in 2026.

But there’s a problem. The valuation is already quite high — too high in my opinion — and has caught up with the recovery narrative. The stock is not trading at distressed multiples any more. It is actually closer to mid-cycle multiples, even though earnings are still depressed. Given their high leverage and still negative FCF, the margin for error is thin.

RYAM currently trades at ~8-9x EBITDA on about $140M earnings. Net debt is above $700M, and FCF is still negative. Management’s strategy is to use the new pricing uptick to expand margins, and maybe become cash flow positive in 2026.

If EBITDA goes up to ~$200M, we could justify a low-to-mid teens price. Anything more than that requires EBITDA approaching $300M. And that needs a near-perfect execution across pricing, costs, and portfolio mix.

That makes further upside from here heavily dependent on how well the company can execute — especially in translating pricing gains into sustained EBITDA increase. The company must also execute cost discipline and deleverage. Such heavy dependence on execution makes the current price less attractive than a year ago. Let's take a look at RYAM ahead of its next earnings release on May 5th.

Rayonier Advanced Materials Business Overview

Rayonier had roughly $1.47B in annual revenue last year, but most of the earnings came from specific segments.

RYAM’s business has five segments, but Cellulose Specialties is the core driver. It typically accounts for ~50-60% of revenue and 70-80% EBITDA (inferred from margin disclosures). Thus, Cellulose is the economic driver for RYAM, producing high-purity cellulose used in applications like cigarette filters, pharmaceuticals, and industrial products.

An important aspect is stickiness. Many of RYAM’s products are customized for specific orders, have long 12-24 months of qualification cycles, and require tight process control. So there’s switching costs and so, contract-based pricing. That helps retain customers. This is also the same segment where the ~18% pricing reset has recently been implemented, so if margins expand, they will do so here.

The rest of the portfolio is different. Cellulose Commodities, Paperboard, and High-Yield Pulp together make up ~40–50% of revenue, but have lower/volatile margins. The reason: margin comes from market-driven pricing, and pricing is tied to global pulp and paper cycles. The company does not have much ability to differentiate on pricing. This is why these lower-margin segments dilute consolidated margins, and also why the overall business cannot command specialty-type multiples.

Rayonier Advanced Materials’ business needs a lot of capital and operational complexity. It runs both kraft and sulfite process types across different feedstocks. That gives RYAM the flexibility to shift production between specialty and commodity. Ultimately, it helps expand margins because they can focus on whatever offers better margins at any point of time.

So RYAM is a hybrid business, with different margins, so its balance sheet amplifies both outcomes. It cannot be valued solely as a specialty business unless they can show that specialties-driven EBITDA growth is both dominant and sustainable.

One news that should be mentioned — because it does give some sentiment support — is that the company recently changed CEOs, and is undergoing a strategic review after an unsolicited interest. Management has indicated it is open to anything, including a sale, or merger, or restructuring. This development comes at a time when the business may be in the early stages of a pricing-led turnaround, although earnings and cash flow are not yet reflecting such a turnaround.

What The Market Is Pricing Today

With $140M EBITDA and 8-9x multiples, RYAM has an enterprise value of $1.1B and trades at around $9. These are not distressed multiples, and they clearly assume current earnings and margins will improve from here. Frankly, the market already thinks EBITDA will move towards the $150-170M range, and is pricing it accordingly.

We can see this from the balance sheet, where the $700M+ net debt makes a large portion of enterprise value belong to creditors, not equity holders. That means, even if EBITDA improves incrementally, that doesn’t fully translate to equity upside.

In other words, the market has already priced for modest improvements. For the stock to move strongly higher from current levels, EBITDA must move so much that some of the debt can be addressed quickly, within 1-2 years. The company also has to go into expansion mode. This means an EBITDA of around $200M range, or even higher.

RYAM Stock Valuation

RYAM stock’s valuation can be framed directly through EBITDA expansion and how that impacts equity.

Under such a framework, the downside case has an EBITDA of ~$120M with a 6x multiple, so enterprise value comes to ~$720M. Subtract net debt of ~$700M from, and we are left with equity value of $0-50M, or a per share price of ~$0-1. In this scenario, the high leverage effectively cripples equity.

If we factor in partial success, we get a base case where EBITDA is ~$200M, and we apply a more positive 8x multiple, so EV becomes $1.6B. Subtracting net debt, we get equity value of ~$900-950M, which gives a per share price of ~$13-15, a 40-60% upside from current levels. This situation can happen if pricing gains translate into margin improvement in Cellulose Specialties, other segments remain anchored — and there is partial rerating.

In an upside, full execution bull case, EBITDA goes up to ~$300M, and we apply a 9x multiple to get enterprise value of $2.7B. Subtracting net debt, we still get an equity value of ~$2.0-2.1B, which translates to a per share price of ~$29–31. This outcome becomes possible with sustained pricing power, stable demand, and very strong cost discipline.

Now, at ~$9 per share (~$400M equity value), the market is effectively pricing in an EV of ~$1.1B, and with an implied EBITDA of $150-170M, it is applying a mid-to-high single digit multiple on forward. That means, moderate improvement is already factored into the price, if not a full turnaround.

This makes the setup highly sensitive to execution. Moving from $140M to $200M EBITDA drives most of the upside, and this can only come from near perfect execution. On the downside, even a modest miss can be amplified by the leverage into very sharp equity compression.

Conclusion

At upcoming earnings, investors need to watch for three signals. First is quarterly EBITDA progression. Can Rayonier Advanced Materials move from ~$140M towards the $180–200M range over the next few quarters? That is the key question. Then we have to watch for free cash flow, and whether there’s a clear shift towards positive cash flow so we know that pricing gains are real and coming through the P&L and balance sheet. Third is net debt reduction, because that leverage is amplifying the earnings stress. Even modest deleveraging can change equity sensitivity. Unless these few things improve, it becomes difficult to justify the current valuation.

At current levels, RYAM is priced for partial success. However, that success is yet to arrive. The three conditions I noted above have to be met, and leverage is also a huge drag on the company. The risk-reward is now skewed towards the downside. For all these reasons, I rate RYAM a Hold at current prices. If the next 1-2 earnings show distinct improvement in all these metrics and clear deleveraging, I may re-rate RYAM.

data.title: Cardinal Energy: Robust Fundamentals, Growth Prospects, Valuation Confirm A Buy
data.description:
Almost a year ago, I made my initial coverage of Cardinal Energy, Ltd. (CRLFF). Its value has already nearly doubled, which justifies my bullish stance. Now, the global economy and energy market have become more volatile than ever amid stubborn inflation and intense geopolitical situations. Yet, CRLFF maintains solid and intact fundamentals while trying to take advantage of potential opportunities in the long run.

Canadian Energy: Safety Despite Volatility

In the latter part of 2025, the energy market has become more volatile amid sharp commodity price swings. This has been evident in the global oil market due to the geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and the offsetting impact of the OPEC oil supply hike that pushed oil prices down to their five-year lows. The impact was also felt by Cardinal Energy Ltd. in its most recent performance. Before I proceed, I'd like to highlight that all numbers except for valuation will be in CAD.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue or petroleum and natural gas revenue amounted to $129.50M, down by 12.4% YoY from $147.78M. This YoY decrease was much lower than in my previous coverage at only 0.2%. Various factors contributed to this notable decrease. One aspect to consider was the continued decrease in WTI Crude Oil price. During the quarter, it dropped to its five-year low of $56.48 per barrel, which was mainly due to the OPEC oil hike. With that, the value of oil CRLFF produced also dropped.

Another consideration was the composition of its production. During Q4 2025, its light/medium crude oil remained the primary component. However, it decreased to 44% of the total versus 48% YoY. Meanwhile, heavy oil rose to 43% versus 38% YoY. Light and medium oil are mostly tied to WTI with some adjustments, so lower WTI prices mean lower light/medium oil revenue. On the other hand, heavy oil is deeply tied to WCS or Western Canadian Select. Note that WCS is priced at a significant discount to WTI due to price differentiation and forex effect. With that, its pricing is typically lower than that of light/medium. It's not surprising that the total revenue decreased that much.

On a lighter note, natural gas had some positive impact due to its strong pricing. In fact, it broke above $5 per MMBtu, which was its two-year high. The only problem was that its impact was limited since it only comprised 9% of the total production. Meanwhile, the operating expenses remained manageable despite the mounting cost pressures amid stubborn inflation. After all, lower oil prices meant lower cost of producing and transporting oil. Of course, these were not enough to offset the decrease in revenues. But these aspects helped CRLFF maintain positive and double-digit profits or margins. In the end, the operating margin remained high at 43.2% despite its decrease from 48.0% YoY.

Middle East Tension: The Good And The Bad

As a business deeply involved in various upstream activities, CRLFF is highly sensitive to oil price swings. And despite the ceasefire deal, volatility is still evident. There are risks associated with a global slowdown or even a recession. After all, higher oil prices mean higher fuel prices, which may exacerbate stubborn inflation. If economic activity slows down as a repercussion, oil demand may also be affected in the process.

On a lighter note, higher oil prices mean higher profits or margins for oil producers. The tension has already sent WTI to its one-year high. Right now, it appears to be under control after returning to $90 per barrel. Even so, it remains much higher on a YoY basis, so there is a huge possibility that it will enjoy higher revenues and margins in H1 2026. More importantly, the tension also led to substitution effect. Some consumers may have switched to other producers to cover their needs. With that, CRLFF may have received or may continue to receive additional oil demand. If the war resumes after the ceasefire agreement, oil prices may rebound, which may push its revenues up again.

Mitigants/Drivers

Amid the offsetting impact of the Middle East tension, I believe it has a lot of risk mitigants and growth drivers. Its high concentration on oil production allows it to benefit from the positive spillovers of the war. I bet the impact will extend to its bottom line earnings.

But a more interesting and overlooked component is its natural gas production. Its prices may have weakened recently, but I believe it offers a lot of growth potential to the company. One aspect to note is the global data center expansion. In 2025-2030, global data center capacity demand is expected to increase by 3.5x. This will require more electricity, which, in turn, will increase the demand for natural gas. If demand exceeds supply, the natural gas price will skyrocket. CRLFF will also benefit from this trend and support revenue growth and margin expansion. Its oil production will receive some spillovers because data centers may need mechanical generators to prevent or mitigate disruptions in case of a power outage. Generators are typically ran by oil or fuel, so CRLFF will enjoy the trend in the long run.

With respect to its finances, its robust liquidity ensures it can sustain its potential expansion to match demand growth in the succeeding years. Its cash and cash equivalents may not be that high at only $4.2B, but it still has $54M receivables to support its liquidity. CRLFF complements it with prudent debt management as it maintains a long maturity profile. In fact, no amount will mature within the year. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.2x ($238.6M/$203M), which means that it only needs 1.2 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. Hence, it still has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To assess its valuation, I decided to use the Dividend Discount Model or DDM. The annualized dividend is $0.52 based on the most recent monthly payout. With that, the dividend growth rate is 10.19% based on the five-year CAGR. I know this model may not be ideal since dividends of CRLFF are volatile. With that, I set its risk premium at 10.0%. I believe this is already very conservative since it is twice the 5.0% market standard. Lastly, I estimated its Beta at 1.02x, which means that the price trend of CRLFF is still in line with the S&P 500, although slightly more volatile. With all this information, I derived a TP or target price of $11.79. This shows that the stock is still reasonably valued with some upside at its current price.

Technicals

CRLFF's technicals are still bullish, although caution is warranted. The price keeps increasing with higher highs and lows, which means that the uptrend still holds. It is also trending above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which shows that the current momentum is still stronger than in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is still increasing above the 200-day SMA, so recent market sentiments have remained optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI warrant some caution because the stock is already overbought. The MACD line is still above the signal line, but the uptrend appears to be flattening, which can tell us that buying volume is decreasing as the stock becomes more expensive. The histogram is still within positive territory but also decreasing, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume but showing more caution. On a lighter note, RSI is still moving in line with the price trend, so we cannot detect any bearish divergence. Profit-taking and dips are possible after overbuying, but more buyers may enter if opportunities for buying appear again.

Conclusion

Cardinal Energy, Ltd. has a lot of growth prospects to sustain its expansion despite geopolitical tension and energy market volatility. This is supported by its robust fundamentals that guarantee its longevity and dividends. Its valuation is also still reasonable, so buying at this level is still ideal. Technicals adhere to it as bullish signals persist despite caution due to overbuying. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apple: The CEO Transition Is A Multiple Compression Catalyst
data.description:
Introduction

I’ve been bearish on Apple Inc. (AAPL) for a while, not based on the business itself, which I find simply formidable, but more on the inherent valuation that exceeds the vast majority of its peers, while it does not do enough, in my opinion, to catch up in the AI game right now. Since my last Sell rating, Apple has seen its stock price only increase by 0.69% while the S&P 500 (SP500) rose by 2.65%. With the announcement of the CEO transition as well as the upcoming earnings, I want to dive into the company to see if it is worth changing my overall rating on the tech giant ahead of its earnings release on April 30th.

The new CEO

As Tim Cook steps down from the CEO role, we can only applaud to its operational success as under his tenure, Apple’s market value grew by nearly $3.7T, surpassing the value created by any other American CEO in total dollars, except Nvidia's (NVDA) Jensen Huang. Revenue also quadrupled during his tenure from $100B to $400B. Cook’s most significant achievement has been the development of the services division which includes the App Store, Apple Pay, Apple Music, and iCloud, that provides a high-margin cushion and evolved into a more than $100B annual business. Cook also oversaw the creation of the wearables category that solidified the company’s position as a health and lifestyle brand.

Looking ahead to John Ternus’s tenure, it is a shift from operational and supply chain mastery and more toward engineering and product architecture. He has been a perennial insider as he joined the design team in 2001 and has overseen hardware engineering of every major product line, including the iPhone, iPad, Mac and AirPods. His leadership style has been described as decisive and instant-led, which is relatively different than Cook’s style. His style has been compared to Steve Jobs and this is viewed as an essential by the Board to navigate the current industry transition driven by AI. The transition is also involving a broader shift of the C-suite with the main goal to consolidate power within the engineering organization as Apple doubles down on integrated hardware and silicon capabilities.

Though I believe that Ternus is probably the right man for the job (at the end of the day, the smart people at Apple are sure to make the right decision), his tenure will start at a time when Apple’s stock trades at a high premium and many leadership transitions of the sort have led to multiple compressions. This will especially come if the new CEO cannot immediately resolve concerns about the stalled AI strategy.

Earnings Preview

First, I’ll look at the potential gross margin compression, as Apple guided for an up to 49% gross margin for its fiscal Q2, but the firm flagged rising memory costs and component shortages. If gross margins come in at the lower end or even below the 48% floor, it will signal us that the cost structure is less insulated from the AI CapEx cycle than optimistic investors expect.

I’ll also look at the potential services growth deceleration as during Q1, services grew 14% Y/Y, but if the Q2 numbers come in below that range, mainly if the App Store growth decelerates amid the Supreme Court ruling forcing external payment links, it might undermine the highest-multiple segment in the firm’s portfolio. It will be relevant to see if the firm mentions anything about the Supreme Court decision.

On the supply chain side, Apple’s guidance has assumed the current tariff landscape and no worsening, but with the 145% tariffs on Chinese imports and the reciprocal tariffs hitting India, Vietnam, and other diversification destinations, any forward guidance acknowledging margin pressure would be significant. Morgan Stanley has estimated Apple would need to raise U.S. prices by nearly 18% to fully offset tariff costs.

Of course, I’ll look for the comments on the CEO transition as it will be Cook’s last earnings call as the CEO. If the call is purely about Cook's farewell with no vision articulation by Ternus, then it might reinforce the fact that the transition is undergoing without a clear strategic pivot.

And finally, I’ll look at any commentary on the competition landscape in China, as Apple has been facing the rising threat that is Huawei. To give a backdrop, China is the third-largest market for Apple as iPhone revenue surged 23% in Q1, but it was effectively a launch quarter. Q2 is seasonally weaker and will be the first real test of whether demand was pulled forward or sustainable.

Valuation

As I mentioned earlier, my biggest bearish stance is about Apple’s valuation, which currently has a P/E Non-GAAP of 34.52, higher than Meta (META) at 22.59 and Microsoft (MSFT) at 27.18, despite these two firms growing their net income by 34.11% and 20.92% CAGR, respectively, over the past 3 years. Apple’s effective re-rating that forced a multiple expansion has been based on the services segment, and the main question is whether this is sustainable.

The growth rate has been decelerating in recent years and the high-margin part of this would be the Google search licensing deal, which is estimated to bring $20 to $25B annually, but it is under direct threat from the DOJ antitrust case against Google. Any change to this arrangement would create a massive revenue gap in the services segment, potentially depressing valuation multiples. Though I’ll say that the firm's FCF yield is relatively satisfactory despite the heightened share price, as it stands at 3.1%.

Risks to my bearish take

My bear case assumes plainly that the high valuation and strategic gaps will lead to a market correction, though Apple possesses a massive and loyal installed base of over 2.5B active devices. This creates an unparalleled recurring revenue engine for services that may remain durable even if hardware growth fluctuates.

And despite component cost inflation, Apple is set to potentially reach a 50% gross margin within 2 years, thanks to the shift toward high-margin Services and the use of in-house silicon to lower hardware costs.

On the leadership side, Ternus is apparently a product architect, unlike the operationally focused Tim Cook. Therefore, his leadership could boost product development and restore a more decisive, Jobs-era focus on breakthrough innovation rather than incremental updates.

Conclusion

John Ternus will inherit a company that is at the height of its power while at the same time its most vulnerable strategic crossroads. The foundation laid by Tim Cook provides the resources necessary to potentially navigate the AI transition. Though the current 34x earnings multiple assumes a level of execution perfection that Apple has failed to demonstrate in its software intelligence division over the past 2 years. This valuation problem persists because of the timing of this transition, and I would not be surprised if the multiple compress through the transition. I rate Apple a Sell.
